Since this is your first exercise, I wanted you to start off with pegboard one, just to make sure you have a good understanding of how the camera moves. Moving the camera in robotics is a three-step process. First, you move your arms to the point of interest, but keeping them within the range of the screen. Secondly, you engage the foot pedal, keeping your foot on it, and then you pull yourself closer. So here you'll hear the foot pedal, you don't really have to look at the video. When you're pulling back, you do the opposite, you push yourself away. So here, hands move in, you engage the foot pedal, you move yourself in. And then you push yourself out. Hands move in, hit the foot pedal, pull yourself in. And then hit the foot pedal, pull yourself away. And so on and so forth. For those of you using the robot for the first time, I will do this exercise just a few times. You don't have to train to proficiency before moving over to bag board two.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of the ender wrist to pick up rings from pegs at multiple vertical levels. The left arm slides the ring off the peg using the instrument tips, hands it to the other arm, and returns to the horizontal peg. The surgeon twists fingers to align the ring with the peg, emphasizing coordinated arm movement and stability by keeping forearms on the armrest. The camera view remains stable as the arms move together, and the surgeon pushes up to get an overview of the flashing ring on the third level.

The video demonstrates common mistakes during the task, including excessive force when pulling the ring off the peg, indicated by the left arm turning red. It shows poor economy of motion with unnecessary movements before reaching the target, and overreaching when the peg is close instead of navigating the camera. The handoff error is highlighted, where the left arm fails to release the ring promptly during transfer to the other arm.

The last one is picking up the ring with the wrong flashing arm. I've seen a lot of people get overwhelmed sometimes with this task and just picking it up with the wrong arm. All you need to do is do an extra pass which would generally lengthen your time but it's not a grave mistake. This is one of the tasks that you have to train to proficiency, and the expert proficiency levels have been posted to our page. Since this is a task that we will train to proficiency, I wanted to go over the metrics real quick. So first thing, forget about time and economy of motion. Those are the last things you're going to correct. The first green ticks you should start to see are in the instrument drops, the instruments out of view, and the instrument collisions. then you will start to find an improvement in economy of motion. If you persist to be below economy of motion after those, you should think of more efficient straight lines to move into, like the tips that I told you in the exercise or in the video that you should get an overview off where the rings are before moving in. And finally, if all that shows green ticks, then you should start moving a little bit quickly in order to lessen your time. If you start concentrating on time early on in the task you will drop all other metrics right from the beginning.
